journal-title-group.journal-title: Journal of the American Heart Association: Cardiovascular and Cerebrovascular Disease

title-group.article-title: Association of Pre‐COVID Fitness With Post‐COVID Fitness and Long COVID in the Cooper Center Longitudinal Cohort Study
title-group.alt-title: Cardiorespiratory Fitness Before and After COVID

aff.institution: Zuckerberg San Francisco General, University of California San Francisco
Kenneth H Cooper Institute at Texas Tech University Health Sciences Center
Infectious Diseases and Global Medicine at Zuckerberg San Francisco General, University of California San Francisco

author-notes.corresp: * Correspondence to: Matthew S. Durstenfeld, MD, MAS, Division of Cardiology, UCSF at Zuckerberg San Francisco General Hospital, 1001 Potrero Avenue, 5G8, San Francisco, CA 94110. Email: matthew.durstenfeld@ucsf.edu

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2025 The Author(s). Published on behalf of the American Heart Association, Inc., by Wiley.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/ License, which permits use and distribution in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited, the use is non‐commercial and no modifications or adaptations are made.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: Cross‐sectional studies suggesting that SARS‐CoV‐2 infection and long COVID are associated with reduced cardiorespiratory fitness (CRF) lack preinfection CRF measures. The objective of this study was to determine the association of SARS‐CoV‐2 infection and long COVID with change in CRF.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Cooper Center Longitudinal Study is a cohort study based at the Cooper Clinic, a preventive medicine clinic in Dallas, Texas; we included adults ages 20 to 74 years old with CRF assessed at least twice between 2017 and 2023. COVID status was defined as “prepandemic” (2 CRF measures pre‐2020), “uninfected” (no self‐reported COVID), “recovered” (self‐reported COVID with symptoms ≤3 months), or “long COVID” (self‐reported COVID with symptoms >3 months). CRF was estimated in metabolic equivalents via a maximal modified Balke treadmill protocol.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: We included 4005 participants (mean age: 51.8 years, 26.8% women), of whom, 1666 (41.6%) reported COVID and 80 (4.8% of infected) reported long COVID along with 1826 uninfected and 513 pre‐pandemic controls. At baseline, those who later developed long COVID had lower CRF (10.0 metabolic equivalents, 11.1 recovered, 10.7 uninfected, 11.3 prepandemic; P<0.001). All groups exhibited minor decreases in CRF (~0.2 metabolic equivalents; P<0.001 for each). CRF decreased slightly more among the infected (−0.1 metabolic equivalents greater decrease [95% CI, −0.1 to 0.0]; P=0.02) but not by long COVID status (P=0.10).

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: Pre‐COVID fitness, on average, is lower among people who developed long COVID. COVID does not greatly accelerate age‐related declines in CRF, even among some with long COVID, although few included participants had severely disabling long COVID. Future longitudinal research will clarify if differences in CRF by infection status emerge over longer follow‐up.

institution-wrap.institution: National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute

fn.p: This study was presented at the American Heart Association's EPI|Lifestyle meeting, March 18–21, 2024, in Chicago, IL.
This article was sent to Yen‐Hung Lin, MD, PhD, Associate Editor, for review by expert referees, editorial decision, and final disposition.
For Sources of Funding and Disclosures, see page 9.

list-item.p: Reduced cardiorespiratory fitness may be a risk factor for development of long COVID.
On average, those with mild long COVID did not experience a greater decline in exercise capacity or physical activity compared with uninfected, recovered, or prepandemic controls.

sec.title: What Question Should Be Addressed Next?

list-item.p: What distinguishes those who experience a significant loss in cardiorespiratory fitness after COVID from those who do not, and can those factors inform strategies to prevent or treat long COVID?

body.p: Cardiorespiratory fitness (CRF) is an important determinant of lifespan and healthspan. 1 Even a small change in CRF trajectory has meaningful public health implications. 2 Multiple studies have demonstrated cross‐sectional associations between COVID or COVID with persistent symptoms (long COVID) and reduced CRF. 3 , 4 A major limitation of the published literature is a lack of pre‐COVID assessments of CRF to assess whether COVID or subsequent long COVID are associated with different trajectories of CRF independent of pre‐COVID CRF. Most published studies examining post‐COVID CRF have been restricted to those with severe acute COVID requiring hospitalization or intensive care. Finally, the use of historical cohorts or population norms as control groups eliminates the ability to distinguish the impact of COVID on CRF from the impact of pandemic‐related changes in health behaviors on CRF. As a result, whether lower CRF at baseline is a risk factor for developing long COVID or whether long COVID causes a population‐level reduction in CRF remains uncertain, especially among those who experience mild acute COVID and those with milder, nondisabling long COVID.

Therefore, we studied trajectories of CRF before and after COVID and development of long COVID within the CCLS (Cooper Center Longitudinal Study), which includes individuals who receive preventive clinical care with routine assessment of CRF. We hypothesized that COVID would be associated with a greater reduction in CRF compared with uninfected individuals and that long COVID would be associated with a greater reduction in CRF in comparison to both uninfected and recovered participants.

sec.title: Data Availability
sec.p: The CCLS's data are not publicly available. A scientific data request may be submitted to the Kenneth H. Cooper Institute's Scientific Review Board Committee for review. The committee meets regularly to assess the merits of all requests.

sec.title: Study Design, Setting, and Participants
sec.p: The CCLS is an observational, prospective study initiated in 1970 to assess the relationship between cardiorespiratory fitness, preventive medicine, and long‐term health and wellness. 5 It includes patients seen at a private preventive medicine clinic, the Cooper Clinic, in Dallas, TX, who consent to participate in the cohort study. Adult cohort participants 20 to 75 years old were considered eligible for this analysis if they completed at least 2 clinic visits between January 2017 and June 2023 with CRF assessed including at least 1 pre‐COVID visit and 1 visit after the COVID questionnaire was available (January 2021). We excluded those with history of myocardial infarction, stroke, cancer, those with body mass index (BMI) <18.5 kg/m2, and pregnant individuals. From among 7434 CCLS participants with at least 2 preventive visits with CRF assessments from 2017 to 2023, we included 4005 participants (Figure 1).

caption.title: Participant inclusion diagram.
caption.p: On the left we present a graphical representation of the group definitions. On the right we present a Consolidated Standards of Reporting Trials diagram. Among 7434 individuals, 4848 did not have any of our exclusion criteria, and a further 843 were excluded for missing covariates or <2 included exams, resulting in a sample of 4005 individuals. Of these 1666 self‐reported COVID including 1586 who reported symptoms lasting <3 months (“recovered”) and 80 who reported symptoms lasting greater than 3 months (“long COVID”). Of the 2339 who reported no history of COVID, 513 had 2 exercise tests completed from 2017 to 2019 and were considered as “prepandemic” and the remaining 1826 who completed their first follow‐up exam in 2020 or later were classified as “uninfected.” *456 who completed a clinic visit after January 2021 without completing a treadmill test were compared with those who did complete a treadmill test for a sensitivity analysis examining potential selection bias. MET indicates metabolic equivalent.

sec.title: Exposures
sec.p: Questions related to COVID were added to the Cooper Clinic medical history questionnaire in January 2021 (Figure S1). The primary exposure of COVID‐19 was elucidated with yes/no, date of diagnosis, and duration of symptoms. The primary exposure was self‐reported COVID and survey‐derived presence of long COVID symptoms at the time of CRF assessment, which was determined from the entire sequence of study visits. Because there were no validated questionnaires to evaluate long COVID in 2020, survey questions were developed based literature published at the time of survey development in 2020 6 with input from clinicians. Participants were specifically asked if they experienced any of the following persistent symptoms: fatigue, brain fog, cough, shortness of breath, chest pain, and joint pain with duration of persistent symptoms grouped into 2 categories based on the consensus definition of long COVID 7 : ≤3 months and >3 months. Among those who reported never having COVID during the study period, we used the first follow‐up exam in place of the first post‐COVID exam and classified them as “prepandemic” controls if their first follow‐up exam was between January 2017 and December 2019 and presumed uninfected contemporary controls (shortened to “uninfected” throughout the remainder of the article) if their first follow‐up exam occurred in 2020 or later. Questions assessing severity of acute disease included hospitalization, prescription, or intravenous medication for COVID and the occurrence of any serious acute complications of COVID (ventilator, blood clot in lungs, and blood clot in legs).

sec.title: Outcomes
sec.p: Participants completed at least 2 exercise treadmill tests using the modified Balke protocol. 8 , 9 The primary outcome was CRF estimated as final workload in metabolic equivalents (METs; 1 MET=3.5 mL O2 uptake/kg per min) from the final treadmill speed and grade, which is highly correlated with maximal oxygen uptake in both men (r=0.92) 10 and women (r=0.94). 11 The secondary outcome was treadmill exercise time. We also assessed maximal heart rate and heart rate recovery at 1 minute. CRF was further classified into 5 categories based on age and sex (lowest to highest fitness). 9

sec.title: Other Variables
sec.p: Potential confounders included age, sex, BMI, current smoking, and facemask use during the stress test; we adjusted for these in our primary models. Other variables recorded include self‐reported race or ethnicity and personal medical history including diabetes, hypertension, anxiety, depression, and physical activity. As previously described, leisure time physical activity was based on reported weekly type, frequency, and duration of activity over the past 3 months. 9 , 12 Frequency and duration were converted to minutes of activity per week, multiplied by an estimated MET value from the Compendium of Physical Activity values, 13 and summed across all activities to total physical activity in MET‐minutes per week (MET‐minutes/week).

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Baseline characteristics were summarized by COVID status (prepandemic, uninfected, recovered, and long COVID) throughout the study period. We used likelihood ratio chi‐square statistics to test group differences in nominal variables and rank‐sum statistics to test group differences in ordinal and continuous variables. We used linear mixed effects regression models to estimate adjusted fitness differences between groups. A 4‐level COVID group factor (prepandemic, uninfected, recovered, long COVID), 2‐level (pre/post) time factor, and the group×time interaction were the primary fixed effects in these models. Random intercepts and slopes (over time) by participant were included to account for correlations of repeated fitness measurements and changes among repeated measurements. Fixed covariates included age, sex, current smoking, BMI, and use of a facemask during the treadmill test. We also fit the same models with a 2‐level group factor (uninfected/infected) and 3‐level factor (uninfected, recovered, long COVID). Similar models were fit (1) in the subgroup with complete and concurrent data on physical activity (with additional adjustment for physical activity), (2) after excluding participants who wore a facemask during their treadmill test (without adjustment for use of a facemask), and (3) after excluding current smokers (without adjustment for current smoking). All analyses were programmed in SAS/STAT, version 9.4 (SAS Institute, Inc., Cary, NC, USA).

sec.title: Study Approval and Reporting
sec.p: Institutional review board approval was granted by the Cooper Institute. All participants provided signed informed consent. The study is reported in accordance with the Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology guidelines.

sec.title: Role of the Funding Source
sec.p: This research project did not have external funding—staff salaries for the research staff were paid by the Cooper Institute. External investigators including Dr Durstenfeld, Dr Pettee Gabriel, Dr Berry, and Dr Peluso are funded by the National Institutes of Health.

sec.title: RESULTS
sec.p: The study population had a mean age of 52 years including 27% women (Table 1); 87% identified as White, 2% Black, and 6% Hispanic. A total of 1666 (41.6%) of the sample reported COVID; only 18 individuals (1.1%) were hospitalized, and none required mechanical ventilation. Most participants (3409; 85.1%) were unvaccinated at the time of their baseline exam, whereas most (2409; 65%) were vaccinated by the time of their first post‐COVID exam.

caption.p: Baseline Characteristics by Eventual COVID Status

tr.td: Heart rate recovery at 1 min, bpm* , ‡

fn.p: The numbers missing are: race/ethnicity 44, diabetes 120, hypertension 75, anxiety 76, depression 62, unexplained exertional dyspnea 81, physical activity volume 255, moderate+vigorous physical activity 255, maximum heart rate 1, heart rate recovery at 1 min 4).

sec.p: Among those with COVID, 1482 (89.0%) reported resolution of symptoms within 3 weeks, 104 (6.2%) reported symptoms lasting 3 weeks to 3 months, and 80 (4.8%) reported symptoms lasting >3 months. Of those with persistent symptoms, common symptoms included fatigue (40%), brain fog (39%), shortness of breath (24%), cough (24%), joint pain (19%), and chest pain (10%). The median (interquartile range [IQR]) number of symptoms reported from this list was 1 (1–2).

sec.title: Baseline Characteristics and Subsequent Long COVID
sec.p: At baseline, individuals who subsequently reported long COVID symptoms were more likely to be female (37.5% versus 24.8% recovered, 29.1% uninfected, and 23.6% prepandemic; P=0.002). At baseline, those who subsequently developed long COVID were less physically active, exercising a median 795 MET‐minutes/week (IQR, 128–1448), compared with a median 1200 among recovered (IQR, 630–1964), 1169 (IQR, 510–2032) among uninfected, and 1080 (IQR, 519–1898) among prepandemic participants (P=0.009). Hypertension was more common among people who developed long COVID (35% versus 21% in other groups, P=0.03), and the trend toward higher pre‐COVID BMI among those with long COVID was not statistically significant (Table 1; P=0.21). Few participants reported anxiety or depression at baseline with no statistically significant differences (P=0.29 and P=0.06, respectively).

At baseline, unadjusted mean CRF was 1 MET lower among those who went on to develop long COVID (10.0 METs [SD, 2.2]) compared with recovered participants (11.1 METs [SD, 2.2]), uninfected individuals (10.7 METs [SD, 2.1], and prepandemic individuals 11.3 METs [SD, 2.0]; P<0.001). Results were similar stratified by sex (Figure 2) and with treadmill time (Table 1). Furthermore, at baseline, individuals who developed long COVID were more likely to be low fit for age and sex (18.8% long COVID, 7.1% recovered, 8.9% uninfected, and 4.3% prepandemic; P<0.001).

caption.title: Baseline and follow‐up cardiorespiratory fitness by COVID status stratified by sex.
caption.p: Unadjusted mean cardiorespiratory fitness at baseline was lowest among participants who went on to develop long COVID. Cardiorespiratory fitness continued to be lower among people with long COVID. Mean changes in CRF in all groups are small (~0.2 METs). CRF indicates cardiorespiratory fitness; and MET, metabolic equivalent.

sec.title: CRF and Self‐Reported Physical Activity at Follow‐Up (Post‐COVID)
sec.p: At the first post‐COVID visit (or first follow‐up visit for uninfected participants), CRF was approximately 1 MET lower among people with long COVID (9.8 METS [SD, 2.2] versus 10.8 [SD, 2.2] among recovered participants versus 10.5 [SD, 2.2] among uninfected participants versus 11.2 [SD, 2.0] among prepandemic participants; P<0.001; Table 2). Results were similar using treadmill time. Analogous to preinfection trends, self‐reported physical activity was lower among those with long COVID at 880 MET‐minutes/week (IQR, 128–1800) compared with recovered participants (1223; IQR, 633–2144), uninfected participants (1245; IQR, 615–2189), and prepandemic participants (1164; IQR, 638–2002); the comparison among groups remained statistically significant (P=0.03).

caption.p: Participant Characteristics at Follow Up by COVID Status

tr.td: Heart rate recovery at 1 min, bpm*

fn.p: The numbers missing are: race 44, diabetes 31, hypertension 45, anxiety 69, depression 49, unexplained exertional dyspnea 10, race/ethnicity 44, physical activity volume 214, moderate+vigorous physical activity 214, heart rate recovery at 1 min 7).

sec.title: Change in CRF
sec.p: The median time between CRF assessments was 1.5 years (IQR, 1.1–2.1), with medians of 1.3 for prepandemic, 1.6 for contemporary uninfected, 1.4 for recovered, and 1.5 for long COVID, respectively. For those with COVID, the time from COVID to first post‐COVID treadmill test was 8.6 months (IQR, 4.8–14.1). All 4 groups exhibited statistically significant but minor decreases in mean CRF (~0.2 METs; Figure 2). Adjusted for age, sex, BMI, smoking, and facemask use during the test, there was a greater decline in CRF among people with COVID versus without COVID (−0.07 METs greater decrease [95% CI, ‐0.13 to −0.01]; P=0.02; Table S1). In the 4‐group comparison (Table 3), the individual group differences of interest were long COVID compared with recovered: −0.01 METs (95% CI, −0.21 to 0.20; P=0.94), long COVID compared with uninfected: −0.09 METs (95% CI, −0.29 to 0.12; P=0.41), and long COVID compared with prepandemic: −0.04 (95% CI, −0.26 to 0.17; P=0.70). The group × time interaction was not statistically significant (P=0.10).

caption.p: Adjusted Cardiorespiratory Fitness Estimates Before and After COVID by COVID Groups*

tr.td: Difference Compared with long COVID (95% CI)

fn.p: Prepandemic is defined as no self‐reported COVID and first follow‐up treadmill test completed before 2020. Uninfected is defined as no self‐reported COVID at any visit after the survey was available in January 2021 and follow up test after January 2020. Recovered is defined as self‐reported COVID with no persistent symptoms beyond 3 months. Long COVID is defined as self‐reported COVID with persistent symptoms greater than 3 months, consistent with the World Health Organization definition.
Adjusted for age, sex, current smoking, body mass index, and mask use during the exercise test.

sec.title: Sensitivity Analyses
sec.p: We considered other factors that might affect our findings: (1) results were similar in a 3‐factor exposure combining all uninfected participants; (2) we adjusted for concurrently reported physical activity volume in the subgroup of 3757 participants with these data; (3) we refit the main model after excluding 509 treadmill tests during which the participant wore a facemask; (4) we refit the main model after excluding 268 current smokers; and (5) we excluded those whose only post‐COVID visit was within 3 months of COVID (n=137). In all these models, the results were consistent with our main findings: CRF decreased 0.1 to 0.3 METs, with nonsignificant differences between long COVID and the other groups.

We also considered the effect of selection bias from restricting our study sample to those with post‐COVID exercise tests. Adding back those excluded for not having a post‐COVID treadmill test (n=456) to the study sample, people who reported COVID were more likely to not have treadmill test data (14.0% long COVID; 12.1% recovered; 8.8% uninfected; P<0.001), but there was not a significant difference between long COVID and recovered (P=0.59). Mean CRF at the pre‐COVID visit among those without repeat treadmill results were similar to those who participated in the treadmill test (8.9, 9.9, and 9.8 METs) as were median self‐reported physical activity (788, 1190, and 1040 MET‐minutes/week) among those with long COVID, recovered, and uninfected, respectively. Physical activity at the first post‐COVID visit among those without treadmill data did not show that individuals with reduced physical activity preferentially were not tested (1118, 1185, and 1043 MET‐minutes/week among long COVID, recovered, and uninfected, respectively; P=0.26).

Finally, we checked the validity of our classification of participants as having long COVID using the “Unexplained shortness of breath with physical activity” question that was included before and after the COVID questionnaire was added. Unexplained shortness of breath with physical activity was similar pre‐COVID (3.8% long COVID, 2.7% recovered, 3.0% uninfected, and 3.3% prepandemic; P=0.86), but at follow‐up was more prevalent among those with long COVID (5.0% versus 1.0% recovered, 1.1% uninfected, 2.1% prepandemic; P=0.026).

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: Overall, we found that self‐reported COVID is associated with a small but statistically significantly greater decline in CRF compared with those without known COVID, including both contemporary (uninfected) and historical (prepandemic) comparators. We did not find evidence for a greater decline in average CRF among people with long COVID compared with recovered, uninfected, or prepandemic participants. In contrast, pre‐COVID baseline data suggest that lower measured CRF and lower levels of self‐reported physical activity before COVID may be associated with subsequent long COVID.

Our study is unique and benefits from (1) the inclusion of both before and after COVID clinical characteristics, physical activity, and fitness assessments; (2) generally healthy middle‐aged cohort who largely experienced mild COVID; and (3) contemporary and prepandemic uninfected participants. Most prior studies on CRF after COVID predominantly included individuals hospitalized with severe COVID, 3 where confounding from comorbidities and contributions from the post‐intensive care unit syndrome or posthospitalization deconditioning contribute to observed differences in CRF. 14 Observational cohort studies, especially those that recruit from long COVID clinics, may overestimate effects if those with severe long COVID preferentially participate. Finally, most published studies of post‐COVID CRF lack assessments of pre‐COVID CRF. 15 , 16 With those limitations, the existing literature mostly suggests CRF is lower among people with long COVID compared with people who have recovered when assessed cross‐sectionally.

Our study makes the novel finding that lower CRF post‐COVID is primarily attributable to lower pre‐COVID CRF in a middle‐aged, healthy population with mostly mild COVID. To our knowledge, only 4 published studies have pre‐COVID measurements of CRF and consisted of highly select populations. One study included 21 division‐1 soccer players but assessed them only 60 days after recovery, 17 and a second included 49 endurance athletes but excluded those with persistent symptoms 18 ; both demonstrated small reductions in CRF. A study of 103 firefighters found a decrease of 0.7 METs at an average 110 days after mild to moderate COVID, but they estimated that CRF returned to normal around 300 days post infection. 19 Finally, a study of 127 health care workers found a decrease in CRF of ~1 MET among those with COVID‐19 about 1 year after illness versus ~0.2 METs among controls. 20 These studies were too small and homogeneous to identify that low pre‐COVID CRF may be a risk factor for long COVID.

Our findings also suggest that lower pre‐COVID fitness could be a risk factor for development of long COVID. This is consistent with several prior studies suggesting that higher levels of self‐reported physical activity may be associated with lower risk of severe COVID 21 , 22 , 23 and possibly long COVID. 24 This finding complements prior epidemiologic research that did not include functional assessments of pre‐COVID fitness. 25 In our study, the median amount of physical activity done pre‐COVID among those who developed long COVID is still well above recommended guidelines for physical activity. Therefore, our study also provides evidence that long COVID occurs among people who meet guidelines for regular physical activity, not only those who are sedentary.

Our results regarding lower physical activity among those with long COVID suggest that differences in physical activity post‐COVID reported in prior literature may be at least partially explained by lower physical activity pre‐COVID among those who developed long COVID. Other research has noted reductions in physical activity among people with long COVID, although these cross‐sectional surveys were conducted among people with long COVID. 26 , 27 , 28 One study with data collected before COVID found a small decrease in physical activity post‐COVID among those with long COVID. 29 Our study, which also included measures obtained before infection, contradicts these findings and suggests that average self‐reported physical activity after COVID is similar to pre‐COVID, even among those with long COVID included in our study.

Our findings suggest that severely debilitating long COVID is uncommon in the included study population with fewer among those with long COVID in the “lowest fit” category post‐COVID compared with pre‐COVID (13% post versus 19% pre). This may be partially from selection effects as the current study is restricted to individuals who chose to pursue preventive health care and exercise testing before and after COVID. Thus, our study may include few individuals who have experienced a profound decrease in functional status and CRF, especially those with debilitating long COVID, such as those whose symptoms overlap with myalgic encephalitis/chronic fatigue syndrome. 30 , 31 Furthermore, individuals diagnosed with this syndrome may achieve their predicted level of CRF on a single maximal exercise test. 32 , 33 Although those with postexertional malaise (not assessed in this study) may have normal CRF on a single test, they would be expected to have a reduction in physical activity. Contrary to these expectations, our results show that this cohort of healthy, middle‐aged participants maintained pre‐COVID levels of physical activity, even among those with long COVID.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: As an observational study conducted within a private clinic setting, there are several limitations. The first is selection bias related to requiring at least 2 treadmill tests, although pre‐COVID assessments and physical activity data suggest that our results were not likely to be strongly influenced by those who did not have follow‐up treadmill tests. Nonetheless, those with debilitating long COVID or who did not seek preventive care before COVID may also be excluded from our sample. The use of estimated workload, though highly correlated with measured peak oxygen consumption with a metabolic cart, 10 , 11 may introduce measurement error especially without contemporary calibration within the CCLS. We do not have precise dates available for vaccination, which were collected only with the year of vaccination; vaccination may be associated with lower risk and severity of long COVID. 34 The study population is primarily White with access to preventive care, thereby not representative of racial and ethnic minority populations, which have been disproportionately affected by COVID. 35 COVID status was ascertained by self‐report and therefore may be misclassified among those who had asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic infections who did not undergo testing. Long COVID status may be misclassified among those whose symptoms were not included in the questionnaire. Additionally, the Cooper questionnaire does not specifically have questions regarding postexertional malaise or the diagnostic criteria for myalgic encephalitis/chronic fatigue syndrome.

sec.title: CONCLUSIONS
sec.p: In this cohort of community‐dwelling, apparently healthy adults, self‐reported history of COVID (mostly mild not requiring hospitalization) was associated with a slightly greater decrease in CRF over time compared with no history of COVID. We did not find evidence for a significantly greater decrease in mean CRF among people with long COVID. Those who developed long COVID had lower pre‐COVID CRF. Our study suggests that most individuals with mild long COVID maintain similar levels of CRF and physical activity as their pre‐COVID baseline. Encouraging all individuals to engage in routine physical activity to achieve moderate to high fitness levels may help prevent long COVID. Future longitudinal research will clarify if differences in CRF by COVID status emerge over longer follow‐up.

sec.title: Sources of Funding
sec.p: Dr Durstenfeld was funded by National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute K12 HL143961 and is now funded by K23 HL172699. The contents are solely the responsibility of the authors and do not necessarily represent the official views of the National Institutes of Health.

sec.title: Disclosures
sec.p: Dr Durstenfeld has received consulting fees from Merck outside of the submitted work. Dr Berry has received consulting fees from Roche Diagnostics, Astra Zeneca, and the Cooper Institute unrelated to this study. Dr Peluso has served as a consultant for AstraZeneca and Gilead Sciences, outside the submitted work. The remaining authors have no disclosures to report.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: We thank Dr Kenneth H. Cooper, MD, MPH, for establishing the Cooper Center Longitudinal Study, the Cooper Clinic physicians and staff for data collection, and the Cooper Institute for data maintenance. We are also grateful to the CCLS participants.
